notes.note: L. Lucceius, the orator and historian who had run with Caesar (and against optimate cash) for the consulship of 59 BC. His Italici belli et civilis historia (the Social War of 91--88 and the wars of Marius and Sulla) is the work the letter refers to as ``almost finished''; the period after Sulla is what Cicero asks him to put aside in order to write a separate monograph on the Catilinarian conspiracy and Cicero's own exile and recovery.
Cicero's epistola enim non erubescit at section 1 is the famous tag for the freedom of the letter as a confessional vehicle.

Three Greek historians known for separating one war from their continuous histories: Callisthenes of Olynthus (the Phocian war, the Third Sacred War of 356--346 BC), Timaeus of Tauromenium (the war of Pyrrhus in Italy, 280--275 BC), and Polybius (a separate work on the Numantine war of 134--133 BC, lost). The model Cicero asks Lucceius to follow.

leges historiae --- the conventional rule of veracity. Cicero's request that Lucceius set them aside is the classical statement of the distinction between historia (truth) and laudatio (embellishment) and the most explicit invitation in surviving Roman literature for one to be folded into the other.

The fable in Xenophon's Memorabilia 2.1.21--34 (ascribed there to Prodicus) of the young Hercules at the crossroads choosing between Pleasure and Virtue. Lucceius had made a similar self-defence against partisanship in a proem of his own.

The Theban general Epaminondas, mortally wounded at Mantinea in 362 BC, refused to let the embedded spear-point be drawn out until he had been told the Boeotian shield was safe (Diodorus 15.87, Cornelius Nepos Epam. 9). The closing scene of his career, used here as the type of the death that lifts the spirit of the reader.

The flight of Themistocles from Athens after his ostracism (471/0 BC) and the various tales of his return as a refugee at Persian courts (Thucydides 1.135--138, Plutarch Themistocles 22--31). The classic Greek example of the great career of doubtful and varied fortune.
Alexander's two officially appointed artists: Apelles for paintings, Lysippus for sculpture (Pliny NH 7.125, 35.85). The point is the chosen artist, not any artist.
King Agesilaus II of Sparta refused images of himself; Xenophon's Agesilaus, written shortly after the king's death in 360 BC, supplied the lasting picture instead.

Alexander, on visiting the supposed tomb of Achilles at Sigeum on his way through the Troad in 334 BC, said that Achilles was lucky to have had Homer as the herald of his glory (Plutarch Alexander 15).

From Cn. Naevius's tragedy Hector profiscens (third century BC): the famous fragment laetus sum laudari me abs te, pater, a laudato viro --- ``I am glad to be praised, father, by you, a praised man.'' Cicero quotes the second clause from memory; the line was a favourite tag of his.
